# nano-benchmark

> Command-line utilities for benchmarking JavaScript code — ns-scale hot loops and ms-scale per-run operations — with nonparametric statistics and significance testing.

- NPM: https://npmjs.org/package/nano-benchmark
- GitHub: https://github.com/uhop/nano-bench
- Wiki: https://github.com/uhop/nano-bench/wiki
- License: BSD-3-Clause
- Runtime: Node.js (every non-EOL release), Bun, Deno; no `engines` floor pinned
- Module system: ESM only (`"type": "module"`)

## Installation

```bash
npm install nano-benchmark
```

## CLI tool: nano-bench

Benchmarks multiple functions, compares them with bootstrap confidence intervals and significance tests, outputs a styled table.

### Usage

```
nano-bench [options] <file> [methods...]
```

### Arguments

- `file` — JavaScript module to benchmark. If `"self"`, prints its own file path and exits.
- `methods...` — optional function names to benchmark; omit to run every function in the export. A single name runs as a **baseline** — its statistics are reported with no significance test (nothing to compare against in isolation).

### Options

Measurement:

- `-m, --ms <ms>` — measurement time in milliseconds per sample (default: 50). The tool auto-discovers the batch size where one call takes at least this long.
- `-i, --iterations <iterations>` — fixed iteration count per sample (overrides `--ms`, skips calibration).
- `--min-iterations <n>` — minimum iterations per sample (default: 1).
- `-s, --samples <samples>` — number of samples to collect (default: 100).
- `-p, --parallel` — collect samples in parallel (useful for async benchmarks).

Statistics:

- `-a, --alpha <alpha>` — significance level for the confidence interval and tests (default: 0.05 = 95% CI).
- `-b, --bootstrap <bootstrap>` — number of bootstrap resamples for CI estimation (default: 1000).
- `--seed <seed>` — bootstrap RNG seed (32-bit integer). If omitted, a seed is generated and still recorded in the JSON, so every saved run is reproducible; pass it only to pin a specific value.
- `--correction <method>` — post-hoc multiple-comparison correction over the Kruskal-Wallis pairwise comparisons: `none`, `holm` (default), or `bonferroni`. See Statistical methods.
- `-v, --verbose` — show the significance test statistic, critical value, and per-comparison α.

Output:

- `--json <file>` — write a schema-v1 results JSON (raw samples + summaries + metadata). No stdout streaming — a normal run already prints a styled table.
- `--label <label>` — free-form run label recorded in the JSON (e.g. `before-opt`); overrides the file basename as the display name in `nano-bench-compare`.
- `-H, --host` — record `os.hostname()` in the JSON (opt-in; default absent).
- `--host-name <name>` — record a custom machine name in the JSON (overrides `--host`).
- `--histogram` — show a distribution histogram per function (sample shape).
- `--chart <type>` — histogram orientation: `columns` (default, vertical ridgeline) or `bars` (horizontal, side-by-side on a shared axis).
- `--bins <bins>` — histogram bin count (default: auto — scaled to the sample count and capped to the terminal size).
- `--no-emoji` — use ASCII fastest/slowest markers (`F`/`S`) instead of 🐇/🐢 (and `!` instead of the histogram's `⚠`), for terminals with shaky emoji-width handling.
- `-o, --observe` — emit User Timing marks at calibration and sampling phase boundaries, observable via `PerformanceObserver` or DevTools / `node --inspect` traces. Mark names follow `nano-bench/<function-name>/<phase>`. Default: off.

Misc:

- `--smoke` — run each selected function once (`n = 1`) and exit: a pre-flight that validates the module (imports, setup, throws/rejections) before a long collection run. Reports ok/failed with a rough duration per function; the exit code is non-zero if any function throws or rejects. Exits explicitly, so a module holding live handles (servers, watchers) can't hang the check. Measurement/statistics options are ignored.
- `-e, --export <name>` — name of the export to use from the file (default: `"default"`).
- `--self` — print the script's file path to stdout and exit (for Deno/Bun usage).

### Output

A styled table with columns:

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| name | Function name |
| median | Median execution time |
| + | Upper bound of confidence interval (median to high) |
| − | Lower bound of confidence interval (median to low) |
| op/s | Operations per second (1000 / median) |
| batch | Iterations per sample (batch size) |

When two or more functions are measured, a `Significance:` line names the test, α, and (for 3+ functions) the post-hoc method and correction, followed — if the difference is significant — by an N×N matrix of pairwise percentage/ratio differences. The fastest function is marked with 🐇 and the slowest with 🐢 (`F`/`S` under `--no-emoji`). With `--histogram`, a per-function distribution chart follows; with `--json`, a results file is written.

### How it works

1. **Find level**: auto-discovers batch size `n` where `fn(n)` takes ≥ `--ms` milliseconds.
2. **Collect samples**: runs `fn(n)` `--samples` times, collecting timing data (normalized to ms/iteration).
3. **Bootstrap CI**: seeded bootstrap resampling estimates the median and its percentile confidence interval.
4. **Significance test**: Mann-Whitney U (2 functions) or Kruskal-Wallis H + Conover-Iman pairwise post-hoc (3+ functions), with `--correction` controlling the post-hoc family-wise error rate.
5. **Optional outputs**: a distribution histogram (`--histogram`) and/or a schema-v1 results file (`--json`).

The run ends with an explicit `process.exit(0)` once reporting (and the `--json` write) completes, so a module holding live handles (servers, watchers) can't keep a finished run alive.

### Example

```bash
npx nano-bench bench/bench-string-concat.js
npx nano-bench -s 200 -b 2000 -a 0.01 bench/bench-string-concat.js
npx nano-bench bench/bench-string-concat.js plus template   # only these two
npx nano-bench bench/bench-string-concat.js --json base.json --label baseline
npx nano-bench bench/bench-string-concat.js --smoke         # pre-flight: each function once
```

---

## CLI tool: nano-bench-io

Benchmarks slow (ms-scale and up) functions one call per run — no batching — so the run-to-run distribution and its tail stay visible. Same module format as nano-bench; each selected function is called with `n = 1` and awaited. Reports the bootstrap median CI plus p90/p99 tail percentiles, files modified-z outlier notes, and reuses nano-bench's significance tests, histograms, JSON export, and `--smoke`.

### Usage

```
nano-bench-io [options] <file> [methods...]
```

### Run policy

- Default: keep running while runs < `--min-runs` (default: 10) *or* elapsed < `--budget <ms>` (default: 5000), per function; capped by `--max-runs` (default: 1000).
- `-r, --runs <runs>` — exact run count (overrides the stop policy).
- `--stable <pct>` — adaptive stop: run until the bootstrap CI on the median is <= pct% of the median (checked every 10 runs after `--min-runs`; capped by `--max-runs`).
- `-w, --warmup <runs>` — discarded warmup runs per function (fills caches, JIT, connections; default: 0).

### Per-run hooks

The module may export `prepare()` / `teardown()` (named exports, sync or async) — awaited untimed around every run, warmup included. Use them to reset state so every run measures the same thing (drop a cache, open a fresh connection, recreate a temp file).

### Other options

Shared with nano-bench: `-e, --export`, `-a, --alpha`, `-b, --bootstrap`, `--seed`, `--correction`, `-v, --verbose`, `--histogram` / `--chart` / `--bins`, `--no-emoji`, `--json` / `--label` / `-H, --host` / `--host-name`, `--smoke`, `--self`.

### Output

The summary table adds `p90` / `p99` columns and a `runs` column (batch is always 1). After the table: outlier notes (modified z-score > 3.5, slow side only) distinguishing a slow *first* run (caching — consider `--warmup`) from scattered slow runs (interference from other programs), plus a coarse-tail note when a function ran fewer than 100 times (p99 lands on the few slowest runs). Significance testing and histograms work exactly as in nano-bench.

`--json` writes the same schema-v1 results file with `params.mode: "macro"`, per-series `reps: 1`, and `p90` / `p99` added to the stored summary — `nano-bench-compare` reads it unchanged.

### Example

```bash
npx nano-bench-io io-bench.js                      # at least 10 runs and 5 s per function
npx nano-bench-io io-bench.js -r 50                # exactly 50 runs
npx nano-bench-io io-bench.js --stable 5           # run until the median CI width is <= 5%
npx nano-bench-io io-bench.js -w 3 --histogram     # discard 3 warmup runs, show shapes
```

---

## CLI tool: nano-bench-compare

Reads nano-bench results JSON and renders a view/compare table — **recomputing** significance and CIs from the saved raw samples (it never benchmarks). Use it to render one saved run, or to compare a baseline against a new run (before/after) without re-measuring.

### Usage

```
nano-bench-compare [options] <files...>
```

### Arguments

- `files...` — one or more results JSON files written by `nano-bench --json`.

### Options

- `-a, --alpha <alpha>` — significance level for the recompute (default: the first file's recorded α).
- `--correction <method>` — post-hoc correction for the recompute: `none` / `holm` / `bonferroni` (default: the first file's recorded method).
- `--pooled` — compare all series as one k-sample Kruskal-Wallis omnibus instead of pairing by name.
- `-v, --verbose` — show test statistics and critical values.
- `--histogram`, `--chart columns|bars`, `--bins <bins>` — distribution histograms (one chart per series on a shared axis).
- `--no-emoji` — ASCII `F`/`S` markers.

### Comparison modes

- **Paired by name (default)** — partition series by `name`; run one before/after test per name that appears in ≥2 of the files (2 series → Mann-Whitney; 3+ → Kruskal-Wallis within that name). Names appearing once are shown in the summary table but not tested ("not compared"). This is the headline "save a baseline, run a new version, compare" case.
- **Pooled (`--pooled`)** — throw every series into one Kruskal-Wallis omnibus + Conover-Iman post-hoc matrix. Answers "which of these k series differ from which" — but for a before/after it buries the meaningful same-name cells and lets unrelated series perturb the ranks, so it is opt-in.
- **Degrade** — with no shared name (a single file, or disjoint names), paired mode falls back to one omnibus so a single-file render still reproduces the in-run table.

### Comparability banner

The raw samples make recompute exact, but they do not make a cross-machine comparison *valid*. `nano-bench-compare` diffs the recorded environments and warns loudly above the results on any mismatch — runtime/engine version, OS/arch, CPU model/count, total memory — and on `params` (α/samples/bootstrap/correction) or per-series `bodyHash` divergence. `host` and `cpu.speedMHz` are shown as provenance but never trigger a warning. A differing `bodyHash` for a same-named pair means the code changed (the delta may be real); an identical one means any delta is environment/noise.

### Determinism

Each series' CI is reproduced exactly from its saved samples + the run's recorded `params.seed` + its series index + `params.bootstrap` (per-series `mulberry32` streams). The rank tests are deterministic regardless. So a recompute reproduces the original run's verdict and interval rather than silently re-deciding.

### Example

```bash
npx nano-bench-compare base.json                       # render one saved run
npx nano-bench-compare base.json new.json              # before/after, paired by name
npx nano-bench-compare base.json new.json --pooled     # one omnibus over all series
npx nano-bench-compare base.json new.json -a 0.01      # recompute at a different α
```

---

## Results JSON schema (v1)

`nano-bench --json out.json` writes this shape. The **raw per-sample arrays are the source of truth** — significance and CIs are always recomputed from them; the stored summaries are FYI only.

```json
{
  "schemaVersion": 1,
  "tool": "nano-benchmark",
  "toolVersion": "1.0.16",
  "createdAt": "2026-06-03T12:00:00.000Z",
  "label": "baseline",
  "source": {"file": "bench/bench-string-concat.js", "export": "default", "methods": ["plus", "template"]},
  "environment": {
    "host": "nuke",
    "runtime": {"name": "node", "version": "22.11.0", "engine": "v8 12.4"},
    "os": {"platform": "linux", "release": "7.0.0", "arch": "x64"},
    "cpu": {"model": "AMD Ryzen 9 …", "count": 16, "speedMHz": 3600},
    "totalmemMB": 64000
  },
  "params": {"ms": 50, "minIterations": 1, "samples": 100, "bootstrap": 1000, "seed": 12345, "alpha": 0.05, "correction": "holm", "parallel": false},
  "results": [
    {
      "name": "plus",
      "bodyHash": "sha256:…",
      "reps": 100000,
      "samples": [0.000123, "… ms/iteration, full precision …"],
      "summary": {"median": 0.000124, "lo": 0.000119, "hi": 0.000131, "mean": 0.000126, "stdDev": 0.0000044, "opsPerSec": 8064516, "ci": "bootstrap-percentile"}
    }
  ],
  "significance": {"test": "mann-whitney-u", "value": -3.42, "alpha": 0.05, "limit": -1.959, "different": true}
}
```

- `label`, `environment.host` and `params.iterations` (in place of `ms`) appear only when their flags are used; `significance` is present only when ≥2 series were measured.
- `params.seed` is always recorded (the `--seed` value or an auto-generated one), making every run reproducible.
- `results[].bodyHash` is `sha256(fn.toString())` — the per-function comparability guard. Blind spots: closures (source text doesn't capture closed-over values) and imported implementations.

---

## CLI tool: nano-watch

Continuously benchmarks a single function in streaming mode, showing live statistics and memory usage. Runs indefinitely until stopped with Ctrl+C (or until `--iterations` is reached).

### Usage

```
nano-watch [options] <file> [method]
```

### Arguments

- `file` — JavaScript module to benchmark. If `"self"`, prints its own file path and exits.
- `method` — optional method name if the export is an object of functions (same format as nano-bench).

### Options

- `-m, --ms <ms>` — measurement time in milliseconds per sample (default: 500).
- `-i, --iterations <number>` — number of iterations to run (default: Infinity).
- `-e, --export <name>` — name of the export to use from the file (default: `"default"`).
- `--self` — print the script's file path to stdout and exit.

### Output

A live-updating styled table with:

| Row | Columns |
|-----|---------|
| Stats | #, time, mean, stdDev, median, skewness, kurtosis |
| op/s | operations per second for time, mean, median |
| memory | heapUsed, heapTotal, rss |

All statistics are computed using online/streaming algorithms (constant memory):
- **StatCounter**: streaming mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis (Welford's algorithm).
- **MedianCounter**: approximate streaming median (median-of-medians).

A bounded run (`--iterations`) ends with an explicit `process.exit(0)`, so a module holding live handles can't keep a finished run alive.

### Example

```bash
npx nano-watch bench/watch-sample.js
npx nano-watch bench/bench-string-concat.js backticks
npx nano-watch -i 50 bench/watch-sample.js
```

---

## Benchmark file format

The benchmarking tools import a JavaScript module. The module should export (default or named) either:

### Object of functions (for nano-bench / nano-bench-io, or nano-watch with method argument)

```js
export default {
  variant1: n => {
    const a = 'a', b = 'b';
    for (let i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
      const x = a + '-' + b;
    }
  },
  variant2: n => {
    const a = 'a', b = 'b';
    for (let i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
      const x = `${a}-${b}`;
    }
  }
};
```

### Single function (for nano-watch without method argument)

```js
export default n => {
  const a = 'a', b = 'b';
  for (let i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
    const x = a + '-' + b;
  }
};
```

### Key design principle

Each function takes `n` (iteration count) and runs the measured code in a `for` loop. This amortizes function-call overhead over `n` iterations, which is critical for micro-benchmarks where the measured code is faster than the overhead of calling a function.

The batch size `n` is either specified via `--iterations` or auto-discovered by the tool so that one call takes at least `--ms` milliseconds. `nano-bench-io` always passes `n = 1` — at ms scale one call is directly measurable and batching would erase the per-run distribution; keep the loop anyway so the same file works in every tool.

### Async functions

Benchmark functions can return a Promise (be async). The tools detect thenables and measure the time until resolution.

---

## Deno and Bun support

Use `--self` to get the script path, then run with the alternative interpreter:

```bash
# nano-bench
bun `npx nano-bench --self` benchmark.js
deno run -A `npx nano-bench --self` benchmark.js

# nano-watch
bun `npx nano-watch --self` benchmark.js methodName
deno run -A `npx nano-watch --self` benchmark.js methodName
```

For Deno, `--allow-read` is required and `--allow-hrtime` is recommended. Use `-A` for convenience in safe environments.

---

## Statistical methods

### Bootstrap-percentile CI of the median

Used by nano-bench to estimate the median and its confidence interval. For each of `--bootstrap` resamples (sampling the timing data with replacement), it computes a weighted quantile — 0.5 for the median, α/2 and 1−α/2 for the interval bounds — and reports the **mean of those quantiles across resamples**. The RNG is `mulberry32` seeded by `--seed` (or an auto-recorded seed), with a distinct per-series stream, so the CI is reproducible exactly on recompute. Before the bootstrap completes, an exact order-statistic estimate is shown and then overwritten.

### Mann-Whitney U test

Nonparametric two-sample test used when comparing exactly 2 functions; normal approximation, two-sided, tie-corrected. Tests whether the two timing distributions are significantly different at the given `--alpha` level. Does not assume a normal distribution. A single comparison, so no multiplicity correction applies.

### Kruskal-Wallis H test + Conover-Iman post-hoc

Nonparametric k-sample test used when comparing 3+ functions. The omnibus uses a beta approximation for the null distribution of H. If the omnibus is significant, the **Conover-Iman** pairwise post-hoc (Fisher's LSD on the shared ranks, with the Student t critical value approximated by z for large df) identifies which specific pairs differ.

### Multiple-comparison correction

The k(k−1)/2 Conover-Iman pairwise comparisons within one omnibus inflate the family-wise error rate. `--correction` controls it, acting on the per-comparison critical value (`zPpf(1 − α/2)`):

- `holm` (default) — Holm step-down: sort the comparisons by strength and test against progressively relaxed thresholds, stopping at the first failure. Same FWER guarantee as Bonferroni but uniformly more powerful, so it is the default.
- `bonferroni` — single-step; every comparison tested at α/m. More conservative; kept for its familiarity.
- `none` — uncorrected (reproduces the earlier, pre-correction post-hoc behaviour).

The correction touches only the significance path, not the bootstrap CI's α (a CI is one interval, not a family). The 2-series Mann-Whitney case and separate paired-by-name compare blocks are single comparisons (m = 1, uncorrected).

### Distribution histograms

`--histogram` renders each function's sample distribution as an inline terminal chart, on a shared range/bin set so series are comparable. It surfaces multimodality, skew, and outlier tails (GC pauses, JIT warmup) that a single median+CI line cannot show. Bin count is automatic (scaled to the sample count, capped to the terminal), overridable with `--bins`; orientation is `columns` (vertical ridgeline) or `bars` (horizontal). Median (▾) and mean (▿) markers and a multimodality nudge annotate each chart.

### Kolmogorov-Smirnov test

Two-sample distribution comparison test. Available in the library API (`src/significance/kstest.js`), not wired into the CLI.

### Online/streaming algorithms

Used by nano-watch for indefinite monitoring with constant memory:

- **StatCounter** — Welford's online algorithm for streaming mean, variance (M2), skewness (M3), and kurtosis (M4). Numerically stable single-pass computation.
- **MedianCounter** — approximate streaming median using a hierarchical median-of-three structure. Provides O(1) memory approximate median without storing all values.

---

## User Timing API integration

The `nano-bench` CLI accepts `-o` / `--observe`. When set, calibration and sampling phases emit `performance.mark` and `performance.measure` entries to the standard performance timeline. Observers can subscribe via `PerformanceObserver`; the entries are also visible in DevTools / `node --inspect` traces.

Mark and measure names follow the convention `nano-bench/<label>/<phase>`:

- Start mark: `nano-bench/<label>/<phase>:start`
- Measure: `nano-bench/<label>/<phase>` (with the start mark as its start)

Phases are:

- `find-level` — calibration (auto-discovery of batch size).
- `series` — sequential sample collection.
- `series-par` — parallel sample collection (when `--parallel` is set).

The CLI uses each function's exported name as the label, so multiple benchmarks in one run produce distinct entries (e.g., `nano-bench/strings/find-level`, `nano-bench/backticks/series`, etc.).

### Consumer example

```js
import {PerformanceObserver} from 'node:perf_hooks';

const obs = new PerformanceObserver(list => {
  for (const e of list.getEntries()) {
    console.log(`${e.name}: ${e.duration.toFixed(2)} ms`);
  }
});
obs.observe({entryTypes: ['measure']});
```

### Library API

The orchestrating runner functions accept an `observe` option (`boolean | string`):

- `false` / `undefined` — no instrumentation (default).
- `true` — emit marks with the default label `"default"`.
- string — emit marks with the given label.

The option is supported by `findLevel`, `benchmarkSeries`, `benchmarkSeriesPar`, `measure`, and `measurePar`. `measure` / `measurePar` thread it through to the inner `findLevel` and `benchmarkSeries` / `benchmarkSeriesPar` calls so a single `observe` argument produces both calibration and sample-collection entries.

```js
import {measure} from 'nano-benchmark/bench/runner.js';

const fn = n => { let s = 0; for (let i = 0; i < n; ++i) s += i; };
const stats = await measure(fn, {nSeries: 50, observe: 'sum-loop'});
```

### Cost

Marks have a small fixed cost per phase (one `performance.mark` + one `performance.measure` per phase boundary, not per sample). Per-sample timing remains pure `performance.now()` deltas, so observe-mode does not measurably affect benchmark accuracy. The default is off purely to keep the perf timeline buffer empty for users who don't need the integration.

The `nano-watch` CLI deliberately does **not** expose `--observe` because its sample loop is unbounded by default; library users who want to instrument continuous monitoring should manage their own buffer (e.g., `performance.clearMarks()` periodically) via the library API.
